title: Intersection node
description: Find intersection node of two linked lists

examples.input: List A: 1->2->3->4, List B: 9->3->4 (intersection at node with value 3)
examples.explanation: Step 1: Start with headA = node1 (value 1), headB = node9 (value 9). Outer loop: currentA = node1. Inner loop: traverse list B: currentB = node9 (value 9) -> compare node1 vs node9: no match. currentB = node3 (value 3) -> compare node1 vs node3: no match. currentB = node4 (value 4) -> compare node1 vs node4: no match. End inner loop. Step 2: Outer loop: currentA = node2 (value 2). Inner loop: reset currentB = node9. currentB = node9 -> compare node2 vs node9: no match. currentB = node3 -> compare node2 vs node3: no match. currentB = node4 -> compare node2 vs node4: no match. End inner loop. Step 3: Outer loop: currentA = node3 (value 3). Inner loop: reset currentB = node9. currentB = node9 -> compare node3 vs node9: no match. currentB = node3 -> compare node3 vs node3: match found (same object). Return node3.

examples.input: List A: 5->6, List B: 7->8 (no intersection)
examples.explanation: Step 1: headA = node5 (value 5), headB = node7 (value 7). Outer loop: currentA = node5. Inner loop: currentB = node7 -> compare node5 vs node7: no match. currentB = node8 (value 8) -> compare node5 vs node8: no match. End inner loop. Step 2: Outer loop: currentA = node6 (value 6). Inner loop: reset currentB = node7. currentB = node7 -> compare node6 vs node7: no match. currentB = node8 -> compare node6 vs node8: no match. End inner loop. Step 3: Outer loop ends as currentA becomes null. Return null.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n*m)
approaches.summary: For each node in list A, traverse entire list B to check for matching node references. Use nested loops to compare every possible pair of nodes until intersection is found.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Intersection Node of Two Linked Lists Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete solution using nested loops to compare every node in list A with every node in list B.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to finding the intersection node of two linked lists relies on the fundamental property that if two lists intersect, they share the same node object from the intersection point onward. The intuition is straightforward: since we need to find the first common node, we can exhaustively check all possible pairs of nodes from both lists. For each node in list A, we traverse the entire list B, comparing node references (not values, as nodes with same value might be different objects). This ensures we find the exact intersection point where the two lists merge. The algorithm starts by initializing two pointers: one for list A (outer loop) and one for list B (inner loop). In the outer loop, we iterate through each node in list A. For each such node, we reset a pointer to the head of list B and traverse list B completely in the inner loop. At each step in the inner loop, we check if the current node in list A is the same object as the current node in list B (using reference equality). If a match is found, we return that node as the intersection. If no match is found after checking all pairs, we return null, indicating no intersection. The time complexity is O(n*m), where n and m are the lengths of lists A and B, respectively, because for each of the n nodes in A, we traverse all m nodes in B. The space complexity is O(1) as we only use a constant amount of extra space for pointers. This approach is simple but inefficient for large lists, making it suitable for educational purposes to understand the problem before optimizing. Connection to higher levels: This is the complete implementation of the brute force method, building on the core logic of nested traversal. Common mistakes include comparing node values instead of references, which can lead to incorrect results if duplicate values exist, or not handling edge cases like empty lists. To avoid these, always use identity checks (e.g., 'is' in Python) and check for null inputs upfront. Real code pattern: Use while loops for traversal, with careful pointer updates.

examples.input: List A: 1->2->3->4, List B: 9->3->4 (intersection at node with value 3)
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with headA = node1 (value 1), headB = node9 (value 9). Outer loop: currentA = node1. Inner loop: traverse list B: currentB = node9 (value 9) -> compare node1 vs node9: no match. currentB = node3 (value 3) -> compare node1 vs node3: no match. currentB = node4 (value 4) -> compare node1 vs node4: no match. End inner loop. Step 2: Outer loop: currentA = node2 (value 2). Inner loop: reset currentB = node9. currentB = node9 -> compare node2 vs node9: no match. currentB = node3 -> compare node2 vs node3: no match. currentB = node4 -> compare node2 vs node4: no match. End inner loop. Step 3: Outer loop: currentA = node3 (value 3). Inner loop: reset currentB = node9. currentB = node9 -> compare node3 vs node9: no match. currentB = node3 -> compare node3 vs node3: match found (same object). Return node3.

examples.input: List A: 5->6, List B: 7->8 (no intersection)
examples.trace: Step 1: headA = node5 (value 5), headB = node7 (value 7). Outer loop: currentA = node5. Inner loop: currentB = node7 -> compare node5 vs node7: no match. currentB = node8 (value 8) -> compare node5 vs node8: no match. End inner loop. Step 2: Outer loop: currentA = node6 (value 6). Inner loop: reset currentB = node7. currentB = node7 -> compare node6 vs node7: no match. currentB = node8 -> compare node6 vs node8: no match. End inner loop. Step 3: Outer loop ends as currentA becomes null. Return null.

testCases.input: List A: 1->2->3, List B: 2->3 (intersection at node with value 2)
List A: 1->2->3->4, List B: 5->6->3->4 (intersection at node with value 3)
List A: 1, List B: 1 (same node object)
List A: 1->2->3, List B: 4->5->2->3 (intersection at node with value 2)
List A: 1->2->3->4->5, List B: 6->7->8->4->5 (intersection at node with value 4)
List A: 1->2->3->4, List B: 4 (intersection at node with value 4)
List A: 1->2->3, List B: 1->2->3 (different lists, no intersection)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Nested Loop Traversal to Compare Nodes
ladders.desc: Implement the nested loop structure that compares each node in list A with every node in list B.
ladders.explanation: The key sub-routine in the brute force approach is the nested loop mechanism that performs the exhaustive comparison between nodes of two linked lists. The intuition is that to find a common node, we need to systematically check all possible pairs, which naturally translates to an outer loop iterating over list A and an inner loop iterating over list B for each outer iteration. This ensures no pair is missed. The algorithm involves initializing a pointer 'currentA' to the head of list A. In the outer loop, while 'currentA' is not null, we initialize another pointer 'currentB' to the head of list B. In the inner loop, while 'currentB' is not null, we compare if 'currentA' and 'currentB' refer to the same node object (using reference equality). If they match, we return 'currentA' as the intersection node. If not, we move 'currentB' to its next node. After the inner loop completes (i.e., all nodes in B are checked for the current 'currentA'), we move 'currentA' to its next node and repeat. If the outer loop finishes without a match, we return null. The time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n*m) as it involves nested iterations, and space complexity is O(1) since only pointers are used. Connection to level 0: This sub-routine is the core implementation within the full problem, handling the comparison logic. Common mistakes include not resetting 'currentB' to the head of list B for each new 'currentA', which would cause incomplete comparisons, or using value equality instead of reference equality. To avoid these, always reset the inner pointer at the start of each outer iteration and use identity checks. Real code pattern: Use while loops with conditions checking for null, and break early on match.

examples.input: List A: 1->2, List B: 3->2 (intersection at node with value 2)
examples.trace: Step 1: currentA = node1 (value 1). Set currentB = node3 (value 3). Compare node1 vs node3: no match. Move currentB = node2 (value 2). Compare node1 vs node2: no match. currentB becomes null, end inner loop. Step 2: Move currentA = node2 (value 2). Reset currentB = node3. Compare node2 vs node3: no match. Move currentB = node2. Compare node2 vs node2: match found (same object). Return node2.

examples.input: List A: 4->5, List B: 6->7
examples.trace: Step 1: currentA = node4 (value 4). currentB = node6 (value 6). Compare node4 vs node6: no match. currentB = node7 (value 7). Compare node4 vs node7: no match. currentB null, end inner loop. Step 2: currentA = node5 (value 5). Reset currentB = node6. Compare node5 vs node6: no match. currentB = node7. Compare node5 vs node7: no match. currentB null, end inner loop. Step 3: currentA becomes null, end outer loop. Return null.

testCases.input: List A: 1->2, List B: 2 (intersection at tail)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Exhaustive Pairwise Node Comparison
ladders.desc: The central technique of comparing every node in one list with every node in another list.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force approach is the exhaustive pairwise comparison of nodes between two linked lists. The intuition stems from the definition of intersection: two lists intersect if they share at least one common node object. To find the first such node, we need to check all possible pairs, as the intersection could be at any position. This is akin to a Cartesian product of the nodes in list A and list B. The technique involves systematically iterating: for each node in list A (using an index or pointer), we compare it with each node in list B. This ensures that if an intersection exists, it will be detected when we encounter the matching pair. The algorithm can be thought of as a double traversal: the outer traversal picks a node from A, and the inner traversal scans through B to find a match. The key insight is that we must compare node references, not values, because nodes with identical values might be distinct objects in memory. The time complexity is quadratic, O(n*m), due to the nested loops, making it inefficient for large lists but conceptually simple. Space complexity is O(1) as no additional data structures are needed. Connection to level 1: This logic is implemented in the nested loop sub-routine. Common mistakes include assuming lists intersect based on value equality, which can fail if there are duplicates, or not considering that intersection is defined by node identity. To avoid these, emphasize reference checks and validate with examples. Real code pattern: Use two while loops, with the inner loop resetting for each outer iteration.

examples.input: List A: 1->2, List B: 3->1 (intersection at node with value 1)
examples.trace: Pairwise comparisons: Compare node1 (A) with node3 (B): no match. Compare node1 with node1 (B): match found (same object). Return node1. Note: We stop early on first match, but all pairs for node1 are: (node1, node3) and (node1, node1).

examples.input: List A: 4->5->6, List B: 7->8
examples.trace: Pairwise comparisons: For node4 (A): compare with node7 (B): no match; with node8: no match. For node5 (A): compare with node7: no match; with node8: no match. For node6 (A): compare with node7: no match; with node8: no match. All pairs checked, no match. Return null.

testCases.input: List A: 1->2->3, List B: 4->2->3 (intersection at node 2)

ladders.title: Building Block — Traversing a Linked List with a Pointer
ladders.desc: Intermediate concept of iterating through a linked list using a moving pointer.
ladders.explanation: Traversing a linked list with a pointer is a fundamental building block for many algorithms, including the brute force intersection finder. The intuition is that a linked list is a sequential data structure where each node points to the next, so to access all nodes, we start at the head and repeatedly move to the next node until we reach the end (null). This traversal allows us to examine or process each node one by one. The algorithm involves initializing a pointer, often called 'current', to the head of the list. Then, in a loop (while or for), we check if 'current' is not null. Inside the loop, we can perform operations on the node pointed to by 'current', such as comparing it with another node. After processing, we update 'current' to 'current.next' to move to the next node. The loop continues until 'current' becomes null, indicating we've traversed the entire list. The time complexity for traversing a list of length k is O(k), as we visit each node once. Space complexity is O(1) if we only use the pointer. Connection to level 2: This traversal is used in both the outer and inner loops of the pairwise comparison. Common mistakes include not updating the pointer correctly, leading to infinite loops, or accessing 'current.next' when 'current' is null, causing null pointer errors. To avoid these, always check for null before moving and ensure the update step is inside the loop. Real code pattern: Use a while loop with 'current' initialized to head, and condition 'current != null'.

examples.trace: Step 1: current = node1 (value 1). Process node1. Update current = node1.next = node2. Step 2: current = node2 (value 2). Process node2. Update current = node2.next = node3. Step 3: current = node3 (value 3). Process node3. Update current = node3.next = null. Step 4: current is null, stop.

examples.input: List: null
examples.trace: Step 1: current = null. Since current is null, loop does not execute. No nodes processed.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Two Node References
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of checking if two variables point to the same object in memory.
ladders.explanation: Comparing two node references is a basic operation crucial for the brute force intersection algorithm. The intuition is that in linked lists, intersection is defined by node identity, not value equality. This means two lists intersect if they share the same physical node object in memory. Therefore, we need to compare references to determine if two variables point to the exact same object. In programming languages, this is typically done using identity operators (e.g., 'is' in Python, '==' for references in Java, or pointer comparison in C). The algorithm is simple: given two node pointers, say 'nodeA' and 'nodeB', we check if 'nodeA' is the same object as 'nodeB'. If yes, they intersect at that node; if no, they are different nodes even if they hold the same value. This operation is performed repeatedly in nested loops. The time complexity is O(1) per comparison, as it's a constant-time check. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to level 3: This comparison is used during traversal when processing nodes. Common mistakes include using value comparison (e.g., checking if nodeA.value == nodeB.value) instead of reference comparison, which can lead to false positives if there are duplicate values in the lists. To avoid this, always use the language-specific identity operator and understand that nodes are objects with unique identities. Real code pattern: In Python, use 'nodeA is nodeB'; in Java, use 'nodeA == nodeB' for reference types.

examples.input: nodeA points to node with value 5, nodeB points to same node
examples.trace: Step 1: nodeA and nodeB are references. Check if nodeA is nodeB: yes, because they point to the same memory location. Return true.

examples.input: nodeA points to node with value 5, nodeB points to different node with value 5
examples.trace: Step 1: nodeA and nodeB are references to different objects, even though both have value 5. Check if nodeA is nodeB: no, because they are distinct in memory. Return false.

testCases.input: nodeA: node(1), nodeB: node(1) (different objects with same value)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Linked List Node Structure
ladders.desc: The simplest prerequisite: what a linked list node is and how it connects.
ladders.explanation: A linked list node is the basic building block of a linked list, and understanding its structure is foundational for any linked list algorithm. The intuition is that a linked list is a linear data structure composed of nodes, where each node contains two parts: data (or value) and a reference (or pointer) to the next node in the sequence. This creates a chain-like structure, allowing dynamic memory allocation and efficient insertions/deletions compared to arrays. The node structure is typically defined as a class or struct with attributes: 'value' to store the data and 'next' to point to the next node. The 'next' reference of the last node is null, indicating the end of the list. In the context of the intersection problem, nodes are objects in memory, and intersection occurs when two lists share the same node object, meaning they point to the same memory location from some point onward. This concept is crucial because algorithms must compare node identities, not just values. The time and space for a single node are O(1), as it holds constant data. Connection to level 4: This foundation enables the comparison of node references. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with their values or not understanding that 'next' is a reference to another node object. To avoid these, visualize nodes as boxes with a value and an arrow to the next box, and practice creating simple linked lists. Real code pattern: In Python, a node class might be defined as 'class ListNode: def __init__(self, val=0, next=None): self.val = val; self.next = next'.

examples.input: Create a node with value 10 and next as null
examples.trace: Step 1: Define a node object. Set its value attribute to 10. Set its next attribute to null (or None). This node is isolated, not connected to others.

examples.input: Create two nodes: node1 with value 5, node2 with value 7. Link node1 to node2.
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node1: set value=5, next=null initially. Step 2: Create node2: set value=7, next=null. Step 3: Update node1.next to point to node2. Now node1's next references node2, forming a two-node list.

testCases.input: Node with value 2, next points to node with value 3
Node with value 4, next points to null
Node with value 9, next points to node with value 10
Node with value 6, next points to itself (circular)
Node with value 8, next points to node with value 8 (different object)
Node with value 12, next points to node with value 13
Node with value 14, next points to null

approaches.name: HashSet O(n+m)
approaches.summary: Traverse first linked list and store all node references in a HashSet. Then traverse second list checking if each node exists in the set - the first match is the intersection.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Intersection Node of Two Linked Lists Using HashSet
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find the intersection node of two singly linked lists using a HashSet approach.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that if two linked lists intersect, they share nodes from the intersection point onward. By storing all nodes from the first list in a HashSet, we create a lookup structure that allows O(1) checking for node existence. When we traverse the second list, the first node found in the HashSet must be the intersection point because it's the first common node reachable from both lists. The algorithm works because: 1) HashSet provides constant-time membership testing, 2) Linked lists maintain node references, so we can store and compare actual node objects, not just values, 3) The first match in the second list must be the intersection since nodes are unique objects. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize an empty HashSet, 2) Traverse first list (listA), adding each node to the HashSet, 3) Traverse second list (listB), checking if each node exists in the HashSet, 4) Return the first matching node, or null if none found. Time complexity is O(n+m) where n and m are lengths of the lists - we traverse each list once. Space complexity is O(n) or O(m) depending on which list we store (we typically store the shorter list to optimize space). Connection to L1: This full solution uses the key subroutine of HashSet creation and lookup from L1. Common mistakes include: comparing node values instead of node references (incorrect if duplicate values exist), not handling null/empty lists properly, and forgetting to return null when no intersection exists. Real code pattern: def getIntersectionNode(headA, headB): visited = set(); current = headA; while current: visited.add(current); current = current.next; current = headB; while current: if current in visited: return current; current = current.next; return None

examples.input: ListA: 1→2→3→4→5, ListB: 9→8→4→5 (intersect at node with value 4)
examples.trace: Step 1: Create empty HashSet. Step 2: Traverse ListA: Add node(1) to set → {node(1)}. Add node(2) → {node(1), node(2)}. Add node(3) → {node(1), node(2), node(3)}. Add node(4) → {node(1), node(2), node(3), node(4)}. Add node(5) → {node(1), node(2), node(3), node(4), node(5)}. Step 3: Traverse ListB: Check node(9) → not in set. Check node(8) → not in set. Check node(4) → FOUND in set! Return node(4).

examples.input: ListA: 1→2→3, ListB: 4→5→6 (no intersection)
examples.trace: Step 1: Create empty HashSet. Step 2: Traverse ListA: Add node(1) → {node(1)}. Add node(2) → {node(1), node(2)}. Add node(3) → {node(1), node(2), node(3)}. Step 3: Traverse ListB: Check node(4) → not in set. Check node(5) → not in set. Check node(6) → not in set. Step 4: End of ListB reached with no match → return null.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — HashSet Creation and Lookup for Linked List Nodes
ladders.desc: Create a HashSet from a linked list and check for node existence in another list.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine forms the core of the intersection finding algorithm. The intuition is that we need a data structure that allows us to quickly check if we've seen a node before. A HashSet provides O(1) average-case insertion and lookup, making it ideal for this purpose. The key insight is that we're storing node references (memory addresses), not just values, which ensures correctness even when nodes have duplicate values. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize empty HashSet, 2) Traverse the first linked list, adding each node to the HashSet, 3) Traverse the second linked list, checking if each node exists in the HashSet, 4) Return the first found match or indicate no match. Time complexity is O(n) for building the set plus O(m) for checking, where n and m are list lengths. Space complexity is O(n) for storing n nodes. Connection to L0: This subroutine is exactly what's needed in the full problem - we apply it to find the intersection. Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of using a HashSet for membership testing. Common mistakes: Using a list instead of HashSet (O(n) lookup ruins efficiency), comparing node values instead of references, not handling the case where lists share nodes but have different values (impossible in proper linked lists). Real code pattern: def findFirstCommonNode(headA, headB): visited = set(); curr = headA; while curr: visited.add(curr); curr = curr.next; curr = headB; while curr: if curr in visited: return curr; curr = curr.next; return None

examples.input: ListA: 1→2→3, ListB: 4→2→5
examples.trace: Step 1: Create empty set. Step 2: Traverse ListA: Add node(1) → {node(1)}. Add node(2) → {node(1), node(2)}. Add node(3) → {node(1), node(2), node(3)}. Step 3: Traverse ListB: Check node(4) → not in set. Check node(2) → FOUND in set! Return node(2).

examples.input: ListA: 5→6→7, ListB: 8→9→10
examples.trace: Step 1: Create empty set. Step 2: Traverse ListA: Add node(5) → {node(5)}. Add node(6) → {node(5), node(6)}. Add node(7) → {node(5), node(6), node(7)}. Step 3: Traverse ListB: Check node(8) → not in set. Check node(9) → not in set. Check node(10) → not in set. Step 4: Return null.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Using HashSet for Fast Membership Testing in Linked Lists
ladders.desc: The central technique of using a HashSet to check if nodes from one list exist in another.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is that HashSets provide O(1) average-time membership testing, which is crucial for efficiency when comparing elements between two collections. For linked lists, where random access isn't possible, checking if a node exists in another list would normally require O(n*m) time with nested loops. By first storing all nodes from one list in a HashSet, we transform this into O(n+m) time. The intuition: 1) Convert the search problem (finding common elements) into a lookup problem, 2) Trade space for time by storing references, 3) Leverage the fact that node references are unique identifiers. The algorithm essence: Store → Lookup → Return. This works because: A) HashSets use hashing to distribute elements, allowing near-constant time operations, B) Node references serve as perfect keys since each node has a unique memory address, C) The first match during traversal of the second list must be the intersection point in our context. Time: O(n) to build set + O(m) to check = O(n+m). Space: O(n) for the set. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the key subroutine. Connection to L3: This relies on understanding how HashSets work internally. Common mistakes: Using arrays/lists instead of HashSets (O(n) lookup), not considering memory overhead of storing nodes, misunderstanding that we're storing references not values. Real code pattern: visited = set(); for node in list1: visited.add(node); for node in list2: if node in visited: return node; return None

examples.input: Check if any node from [A→B→C] exists in [X→B→Y]
examples.trace: Step 1: Create HashSet from first list: Add A → {A}. Add B → {A, B}. Add C → {A, B, C}. Step 2: Check second list: Check X → not in set. Check B → FOUND in set! Return B.

examples.input: Check if any node from [1→2→3] exists in [4→5→6]
examples.trace: Step 1: Create HashSet: Add 1 → {1}. Add 2 → {1, 2}. Add 3 → {1, 2, 3}. Step 2: Check second list: Check 4 → not in set. Check 5 → not in set. Check 6 → not in set. Step 3: Return null.

ladders.title: Building Block — HashSet Operations: Insertion and Membership Testing
ladders.desc: How to use HashSet's add() and in/contains operations effectively.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the fundamental HashSet operations needed for our algorithm. A HashSet is a collection that stores unique elements and provides average O(1) time for basic operations. The intuition: HashSets use a hash table internally where elements are stored based on their hash code, allowing fast lookups. For our purpose: 1) add(element) inserts an element if not already present, 2) element in set checks if element exists. These operations work in O(1) average time because: A) The hash function distributes elements evenly across buckets, B) Each bucket typically contains few elements, C) Checking within a bucket is fast. Important details: HashSets don't allow duplicates (second add of same element does nothing), order is not guaranteed, and they work with any hashable objects (like node references in Python). In our algorithm: We add all nodes from first list to ensure we have them all, then use 'in' to check nodes from second list. The 'in' operator behind the scenes: 1) Computes hash of the element, 2) Finds corresponding bucket, 3) Checks if element equals any in that bucket. Time complexity: O(1) average for both add and contains. Space: O(n) for n elements. Connection to L2: These operations enable the fast membership testing logic. Connection to L4: This builds on basic iteration concepts. Common mistakes: Assuming HashSet maintains insertion order (it doesn't), using wrong syntax for membership test, not understanding that nodes must be hashable. Real code pattern: my_set = set(); my_set.add(item1); my_set.add(item2); if item3 in my_set: print('Found')

examples.input: Add elements 5, 3, 7 to HashSet, then check for 3 and 9
examples.trace: Step 1: Create empty set: {}. Step 2: Add 5: {5}. Step 3: Add 3: {5, 3}. Step 4: Add 7: {5, 3, 7}. Step 5: Check if 3 in set: Compute hash(3) → find bucket → 3 equals element in bucket → TRUE. Step 6: Check if 9 in set: Compute hash(9) → find bucket → no 9 in bucket → FALSE.

examples.input: Add elements A, B, C to HashSet, then check for B and D
examples.trace: Step 1: Create empty set: {}. Step 2: Add A: {A}. Step 3: Add B: {A, B}. Step 4: Add C: {A, B, C}. Step 5: Check if B in set: hash(B) leads to bucket containing B → TRUE. Step 6: Check if D in set: hash(D) leads to empty bucket → FALSE.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through a Linked List
ladders.desc: How to traverse a singly linked list from head to tail.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is essential for both storing nodes in the HashSet and checking for matches. A singly linked list consists of nodes where each node has a value and a reference (pointer) to the next node. The last node's next reference is null. Traversal intuition: Start at the head (first node), follow next references until reaching null. This is linear traversal since we visit each node exactly once. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize current pointer to head, 2) While current is not null: process current node, move current to current.next, 3) When current becomes null, we've reached the end. Key points: We only have forward movement (singly linked), we need to preserve the head reference to start traversal, and we must check for null to avoid errors. In our algorithm, we do two traversals: one to add nodes to HashSet, another to check for matches. Time complexity: O(n) for a list with n nodes since we visit each once. Space complexity: O(1) for traversal itself (just the current pointer). Connection to L3: We use iteration to add each node to the HashSet. Connection to L5: This builds on understanding what a linked list node is. Common mistakes: Modifying the head reference instead of using a separate current pointer, infinite loops by not advancing current, accessing node.value when we need the node itself. Real code pattern: current = head; while current != null: # process current node; current = current.next

examples.input: Traverse list: 1→2→3→null
examples.trace: Step 1: current = node(1). Step 2: Process node(1). current = node(1).next = node(2). Step 3: Process node(2). current = node(2).next = node(3). Step 4: Process node(3). current = node(3).next = null. Step 5: Loop ends (current is null).

examples.input: Traverse list: A→B→null
examples.trace: Step 1: current = node(A). Step 2: Process node(A). current = node(A).next = node(B). Step 3: Process node(B). current = node(B).next = null. Step 4: Loop ends.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Linked List Node?
ladders.desc: Understanding the basic building block of linked lists.
ladders.explanation: A linked list node is the fundamental unit that makes up a linked list. Think of it as a container that holds two things: 1) Some data (value), and 2) A reference (pointer) to the next node in the sequence. In Python-style terms, a node is typically an object with attributes 'val' (or 'data') and 'next'. The intuition: Unlike arrays where elements are stored contiguously in memory, linked list nodes can be scattered anywhere, connected by these next references. This allows dynamic size and efficient insertions/deletions. The last node has its 'next' set to None/null, indicating the end of the list. Key concepts: Each node is an independent object with its own memory address, nodes are connected in a chain via next pointers, and we typically access the list through a 'head' pointer to the first node. In our intersection problem, we work with node references (memory addresses) rather than just values, which is crucial because two different nodes could have the same value. Time/space: Creating a node is O(1) time and O(1) space. Connection to L4: We need to understand nodes to traverse linked lists. Common beginner mistakes: Confusing node value with node reference, not understanding that 'next' is a reference/pointer not the actual next node, thinking nodes are stored sequentially in memory. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.next = None

examples.input: Create a node with value 5
examples.trace: Step 1: Allocate memory for new node. Step 2: Set node.val = 5. Step 3: Set node.next = None (default). Result: A node containing 5, not pointing to anything yet.

examples.input: Create two nodes: A (value 1) and B (value 2), link A→B
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node A: A.val = 1, A.next = None. Step 2: Create node B: B.val = 2, B.next = None. Step 3: Link them: A.next = B. Now A points to B, forming a mini-list.

approaches.name: Two Pointer with Length Alignment O(n+m)
approaches.summary: Calculate lengths of both lists, then align starting points by advancing the pointer of the longer list by the length difference. Then traverse both lists simultaneously until intersection is found.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Intersection Node of Two Linked Lists Using Two Pointer with Length Alignment
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find the intersection node of two singly linked lists by calculating lengths, aligning pointers, and traversing simultaneously.
ladders.explanation:
The intuition behind this approach is that if two linked lists intersect, they share a common tail portion. By aligning the starting points of both lists so they have equal remaining lengths, we can traverse both lists simultaneously until we find the intersection node or reach the end. This works because after alignment, both pointers will traverse the same number of nodes to reach the intersection (if it exists).

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Traverse list A to calculate its length lenA.
2. Traverse list B to calculate its length lenB.
3. Calculate the absolute difference diff = |lenA - lenB|.
4. Initialize two pointers: ptrA at head of list A, ptrB at head of list B.
5. Advance the pointer of the longer list by diff nodes to align starting positions.
6. Traverse both lists simultaneously, moving both pointers one node at a time.
7. If pointers become equal (point to same node), return that node (intersection).
8. If pointers reach end (both become None), return None (no intersection).

Time complexity: O(n+m) where n and m are lengths of the two lists. We traverse each list twice at most.
Space complexity: O(1) as we only use a few pointers.

Connection to L1: This full solution uses the length calculation and pointer alignment sub-routine from L1 as its core component.

Common mistakes:
1. Not handling the case where lists don't intersect (must return None).
2. Forgetting to advance the correct pointer by the difference (must advance pointer of longer list).
3. Not resetting pointers to heads after calculating lengths.
4. Edge cases: one or both lists empty, intersection at head node.

Real code pattern:
def getIntersectionNode(headA, headB):
    # Calculate lengths
    lenA, lenB = 0, 0
    currA, currB = headA, headB
    while currA:
        lenA += 1
        currA = currA.next
    while currB:
        lenB += 1
        currB = currB.next
    
    # Reset pointers and align
    ptrA, ptrB = headA, headB
    if lenA > lenB:
        for _ in range(lenA - lenB):
            ptrA = ptrA.next
    else:
        for _ in range(lenB - lenA):
            ptrB = ptrB.next
    
    # Traverse simultaneously
    while ptrA and ptrB:
        if ptrA == ptrB:
            return ptrA
        ptrA = ptrA.next
        ptrB = ptrB.next
    
    return None

examples.input: List A: 1→2→3→4→5, List B: 9→8→4→5 (intersection at node with value 4)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Calculate lengths. Traverse A: 1→2→3→4→5, lenA=5. Traverse B: 9→8→4→5, lenB=4.
Step 2: diff = |5-4| = 1. A is longer, so advance ptrA by 1.
Step 3: Initialize: ptrA at head of A (node 1), ptrB at head of B (node 9).
Step 4: Advance ptrA by 1: ptrA now at node 2.
Step 5: Traverse simultaneously:
  - ptrA=2, ptrB=9 → not equal
  - ptrA=3, ptrB=8 → not equal
  - ptrA=4, ptrB=4 → EQUAL! Return node with value 4.

examples.input: List A: 1→2→3, List B: 4→5→6 (no intersection)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Calculate lengths. Traverse A: 1→2→3, lenA=3. Traverse B: 4→5→6, lenB=3.
Step 2: diff = |3-3| = 0. No advancement needed.
Step 3: Initialize: ptrA at node 1, ptrB at node 4.
Step 4: Traverse simultaneously:
  - ptrA=1, ptrB=4 → not equal
  - ptrA=2, ptrB=5 → not equal
  - ptrA=3, ptrB=6 → not equal
  - ptrA=None, ptrB=None → reached end, return None.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Length Calculation and Pointer Alignment
ladders.desc: Calculate lengths of two linked lists and align starting positions by advancing the longer list's pointer.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the heart of the intersection finding algorithm. The intuition is that to compare two lists node-by-node, we need them to start from positions where they have equal remaining lengths. If one list is longer, we skip the extra initial nodes so both pointers have the same distance to travel to reach the end (or intersection).

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Calculate length of list A by traversing from head to tail, counting nodes.
2. Calculate length of list B similarly.
3. Compute absolute difference: diff = |lenA - lenB|.
4. Initialize two pointers at heads of both lists.
5. Advance the pointer of the longer list by diff nodes.
6. Now both pointers are aligned (have equal remaining nodes to traverse).

Time complexity: O(n+m) for traversing both lists to calculate lengths.
Space complexity: O(1) using only a few variables.

Connection to L0: This sub-routine prepares the pointers for the simultaneous traversal in the full solution.

Common mistakes:
1. Not preserving the original head pointers after calculating lengths.
2. Advancing the wrong pointer (should advance longer list's pointer).
3. Forgetting to handle case where diff = 0 (no advancement needed).

Real code pattern:
def alignPointers(headA, headB):
    # Calculate lengths
    lenA, currA = 0, headA
    while currA:
        lenA += 1
        currA = currA.next
    
    lenB, currB = 0, headB
    while currB:
        lenB += 1
        currB = currB.next
    
    # Reset pointers
    ptrA, ptrB = headA, headB
    
    # Align by advancing longer list
    if lenA > lenB:
        for _ in range(lenA - lenB):
            ptrA = ptrA.next
    elif lenB > lenA:
        for _ in range(lenB - lenA):
            ptrB = ptrB.next
    
    return ptrA, ptrB  # Now aligned

examples.input: List A length 5, List B length 3
examples.trace:
Step 1: Calculate lengths: lenA=5, lenB=3.
Step 2: diff = |5-3| = 2.
Step 3: Initialize ptrA at head of A, ptrB at head of B.
Step 4: Since lenA > lenB, advance ptrA by 2:
  - ptrA moves from node1 to node2 (1st advance)
  - ptrA moves from node2 to node3 (2nd advance)
Step 5: Now ptrA at node3, ptrB still at head of B. Both have 3 nodes remaining to traverse.

examples.input: List A length 2, List B length 2
examples.trace:
Step 1: Calculate lengths: lenA=2, lenB=2.
Step 2: diff = |2-2| = 0.
Step 3: Initialize ptrA at head of A, ptrB at head of B.
Step 4: diff=0, so no advancement needed.
Step 5: Both pointers remain at their list heads, already aligned.

testCases.expected: ptrA at node2, ptrB at node4
ptrA at node1, ptrB at node5
ptrA at node3, ptrB at node6
ptrA at node1, ptrB at node2
ptrA at node3, ptrB at node7
ptrA at node1, ptrB at node5
ptrA at node1, ptrB at node7

ladders.title: Core Logic — Simultaneous Traversal with Aligned Pointers
ladders.desc: Traverse two linked lists simultaneously with aligned starting points to find common nodes.
ladders.explanation:
The core insight is that once two linked lists are aligned (have equal remaining lengths), we can traverse them in lockstep, comparing nodes at each position. If the lists intersect, the intersection node will be the first node where both pointers reference the same memory location (not just same value). This works because after alignment, both pointers have the same number of steps to reach any potential intersection point.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Start with two pointers at aligned positions (from L1 sub-routine).
2. While both pointers are not None:
   a. Compare the two pointers (check if they reference same node object).
   b. If equal, return that node (intersection found).
   c. If not equal, advance both pointers to their next nodes.
3. If loop completes without finding equal pointers, return None (no intersection).

Time complexity: O(min(n,m)) in worst case where n,m are lengths after alignment.
Space complexity: O(1) using only two pointers.

Connection to L1: This logic uses the aligned pointers produced by the length alignment sub-routine.

Common mistakes:
1. Comparing node values instead of node references (intersection requires same node object).
2. Not checking for None pointers before advancing.
3. Forgetting to handle case where pointers become None simultaneously (end of both lists).

Real code pattern:
def findCommonNode(ptrA, ptrB):
    while ptrA and ptrB:
        if ptrA == ptrB:  # Compare references, not values
            return ptrA
        ptrA = ptrA.next
        ptrB = ptrB.next
    return None

examples.input: Aligned pointers: ptrA at node3 of list 1→2→3→4→5, ptrB at node8 of list 6→7→8→4→5 (lists intersect at node4)
examples.trace:
Step 1: ptrA=3, ptrB=8 → not equal
Step 2: Advance both: ptrA=4, ptrB=4 → EQUAL! Return node4.
Note: Even though node3 and node8 have different values, we continue until finding same node object.

examples.input: Aligned pointers: ptrA at node1 of list 1→2→3, ptrB at node4 of list 4→5→6 (no intersection)
examples.trace:
Step 1: ptrA=1, ptrB=4 → not equal
Step 2: Advance: ptrA=2, ptrB=5 → not equal
Step 3: Advance: ptrA=3, ptrB=6 → not equal
Step 4: Advance: ptrA=None, ptrB=None → reached end, return None.

testCases.input: ptrA at node2: 2→3→4, ptrB at node5: 5→3→4 (intersect at 3)
ptrA at node1: 1→2→3, ptrB at node4: 4→5→6 (no intersection)
ptrA at node3: 3→4, ptrB at node3: 3→4 (same node, intersection at 3)
ptrA at node1: 1→2, ptrB at node2: 2 (intersection at 2)
ptrA at node5: 5→6, ptrB at node7: 7→8 (no intersection)
ptrA at node4: 4→5→6→7, ptrB at node8: 8→5→6→7 (intersection at 5)
ptrA at node2: 2→3→4→5, ptrB at node6: 6→7→8→9 (no intersection)
ptrA at node1: 1, ptrB at node1: 1 (same node)
ptrA at node3: 3→4→5, ptrB at node3 of different list: 3→4→5 (different nodes with same value)
ptrA=None, ptrB at node1: 1→2 (one pointer already at end)

ladders.title: Building Block — Calculating Length of a Linked List
ladders.desc: Traverse a linked list from head to tail to count the number of nodes.
ladders.explanation:
This fundamental operation is essential for the alignment step. The intuition is simple: to know how much to advance the longer list's pointer, we need to know how much longer it is. We traverse the entire list, counting nodes until we reach the end (None).

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize counter = 0 and current pointer = head.
2. While current is not None:
   a. Increment counter by 1.
   b. Move current to current.next.
3. Return counter.

Time complexity: O(n) where n is list length.
Space complexity: O(1) using only a counter and pointer.

Connection to L2: Length calculation provides the information needed to align pointers for simultaneous traversal.

Common mistakes:
1. Starting counter at 1 instead of 0 (for empty list).
2. Not handling empty list (head = None should return 0).
3. Modifying the original head pointer instead of using a temporary pointer.

Real code pattern:
def getLength(head):
    length = 0
    current = head
    while current:
        length += 1
        current = current.next
    return length

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize length=0, current=node1
Step 2: current not None: length=1, current=node2
Step 3: current not None: length=2, current=node3
Step 4: current not None: length=3, current=node4
Step 5: current not None: length=4, current=node5
Step 6: current not None: length=5, current=None
Step 7: current is None, exit loop, return 5

examples.input: Empty list
examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize length=0, current=None
Step 2: current is None, exit loop immediately
Step 3: Return 0

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Traversing a Linked List
ladders.desc: Visit each node in a linked list sequentially from head to tail.
ladders.explanation:
Traversal is the most fundamental linked list operation. The intuition is that linked lists are sequential data structures where each node contains a reference to the next node. To access all elements, we start at the head and follow the next pointers until reaching None (end of list).

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize current pointer = head.
2. While current is not None:
   a. Process current node (access its data, count it, etc.).
   b. Move current to current.next.
3. When current becomes None, we've reached the end.

Time complexity: O(n) for n nodes.
Space complexity: O(1) using only one pointer.

Connection to L3: Traversal is how we implement length calculation and other list operations.

Common mistakes:
1. Infinite loop by not advancing the pointer.
2. Accessing current.data when current might be None.
3. Losing the head reference by modifying it during traversal.

Real code pattern:
def traverse(head):
    current = head
    while current:
        # Do something with current node
        print(current.data)
        current = current.next

examples.trace:
Step 1: current = node10
Step 2: current not None: process node10, current = node20
Step 3: current not None: process node20, current = node30
Step 4: current not None: process node30, current = None
Step 5: current is None, traversal complete

examples.input: Empty list
examples.trace:
Step 1: current = None
Step 2: current is None, loop doesn't execute
Step 3: Traversal complete (no nodes)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Linked List Node Structure and References
ladders.desc: Understand the basic building block of linked lists: nodes containing data and reference to next node.
ladders.explanation:
A linked list is a linear data structure where elements are stored in nodes. Each node contains two parts: data (the value) and a reference (pointer/link) to the next node in the sequence. The last node points to None, indicating the end. This is different from arrays where elements are stored contiguously in memory.

Key concepts:
1. Node structure: Typically has 'data' field and 'next' field.
2. Head pointer: Reference to the first node in the list.
3. Null/None termination: The last node's next field is None.
4. Sequential access: To reach the 5th node, you must traverse through nodes 1-4.

Visual representation:
Node: [data|next] → [data|next] → [data|next] → None

Why this matters for intersection problem: When two lists intersect, they share the same node objects (same memory locations), not just nodes with same values. This is why we compare node references, not node values.

Connection to L4: Understanding node structure is prerequisite for traversing linked lists.

Common misunderstandings:
1. Thinking linked lists store elements contiguously like arrays.
2. Confusing node value equality with node reference equality.
3. Not understanding that 'next' is a reference/pointer, not the actual next node.

Real code pattern (Python class):
class ListNode:
    def __init__(self, data):
        self.data = data
        self.next = None

# Creating a simple list: 1→2→3
node1 = ListNode(1)
node2 = ListNode(2)
node3 = ListNode(3)
node1.next = node2
node2.next = node3
# node3.next is automatically None

examples.input: Create list: 5→10→15
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create node5: data=5, next=None
Step 2: Create node10: data=10, next=None
Step 3: Create node15: data=15, next=None
Step 4: Link them: node5.next = node10
Step 5: node10.next = node15
Result: node5→node10→node15→None

examples.input: Two lists that intersect: List A: 1→2→3, List B: 4→3 (share node3)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Create nodes for A: node1, node2, node3
Step 2: Link A: node1→node2→node3→None
Step 3: Create node4 for B
Step 4: Link B to SHARE node3: node4→node3 (same node3 from A)
Step 5: Now node3 has two incoming references: from node2.next and node4.next

testCases.expected: Not a proper singly linked list
Three different nodes all with data=5

testCases.input: Intersecting lists: A:1→2→3, B:4→2→3 (share nodes 2 and 3)
testCases.expected: Nodes 2 and 3 have multiple incoming references

approaches.name: Two Pointer Cycle Detection O(n+m)
approaches.summary: Traverse both lists simultaneously with two pointers, and when one reaches the end, redirect it to the head of the other list. The meeting point after redirection is the intersection node.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Intersection Node of Two Linked Lists Using Two Pointer Cycle Detection
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find the intersection node where two singly linked lists merge using O(1) space.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the node where two singly linked lists intersect. The two-pointer cycle detection approach works by having two pointers traverse both lists simultaneously. When one pointer reaches the end of its list, it's redirected to the head of the other list. This creates a scenario where both pointers travel the same total distance (length of list A + length of list B) before meeting at the intersection node, if one exists. The intuition is that by having each pointer traverse both lists (one starting from list A then B, the other from B then A), they cover the same combined distance. If there's no intersection, both pointers will eventually become null simultaneously after traversing both lists. The algorithm initializes two pointers at the heads of both lists. In a loop, it advances both pointers one step at a time. If a pointer reaches null, it's reset to the head of the other list. The loop continues until both pointers point to the same node (intersection found) or both become null (no intersection). Time complexity is O(n+m) where n and m are lengths of the lists, as each pointer traverses at most n+m nodes. Space complexity is O(1) since only two pointers are used. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete traversal logic. Common mistakes include not handling the case where lists don't intersect (infinite loop if not checking for both null) and incorrectly resetting pointers. Real code pattern: def getIntersectionNode(headA, headB): p1, p2 = headA, headB; while p1 != p2: p1 = p1.next if p1 else headB; p2 = p2.next if p2 else headA; return p1.

examples.input: List A: 1→2→3→4→5, List B: 9→8→4→5 (intersect at node with value 4)
examples.trace: Initialize p1 at 1 (A head), p2 at 9 (B head). Step 1: p1=2, p2=8. Step 2: p1=3, p2=4. Step 3: p1=4, p2=5. Step 4: p1=5, p2=null → reset p2 to headA=1. Step 5: p1=null → reset p1 to headB=9, p2=2. Step 6: p1=8, p2=3. Step 7: p1=4, p2=4 → intersection found at node 4.

examples.input: List A: 2→6→4, List B: 1→5 (no intersection)
examples.trace: Initialize p1 at 2, p2 at 1. Step 1: p1=6, p2=5. Step 2: p1=4, p2=null → reset p2 to headA=2. Step 3: p1=null → reset p1 to headB=1, p2=6. Step 4: p1=5, p2=4. Step 5: p1=null → reset p1 to headA=2, p2=null → reset p2 to headB=1. Step 6: p1=6, p2=5. Step 7: p1=4, p2=null → reset p2 to headA=2. Step 8: p1=null → reset p1 to headB=1, p2=6. Step 9: p1=5, p2=4. Step 10: p1=null, p2=null → both null, return null.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Simultaneous Traversal with Pointer Reset
ladders.desc: Implement the core traversal logic where pointers move through both lists with resetting at ends.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine handles the simultaneous movement of two pointers through two linked lists, with the key behavior that when a pointer reaches the end of its current list, it resets to the head of the other list. The intuition is to ensure both pointers traverse the combined length of both lists (n+m nodes) by having each pointer cover one list then the other. This creates a meeting point at the intersection if it exists. The algorithm uses a while loop that continues until both pointers point to the same node or both are null. In each iteration, both pointers advance one step. If a pointer becomes null, it's reassigned to the head of the other list. This ensures that after traversing n+m nodes, pointers align. Time complexity is O(n+m) as each pointer visits each node at most once. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 by being the main loop in the full solution. Common mistakes include resetting pointers incorrectly (e.g., resetting to the wrong head) or not handling the null case properly. Real code pattern: while p1 != p2: if p1: p1 = p1.next else: p1 = headB; if p2: p2 = p2.next else: p2 = headA.

examples.input: p1 at node 1 (list A: 1→2→3), p2 at node 4 (list B: 4→2→3), headA=1, headB=4
examples.trace: Start: p1=1, p2=4. Step 1: p1=2, p2=2 → meet at 2.

examples.input: p1 at node 5 (list A: 5→6), p2 at node 7 (list B: 7→8), headA=5, headB=7
examples.trace: Start: p1=5, p2=7. Step 1: p1=6, p2=8. Step 2: p1=null → reset to headB=7, p2=null → reset to headA=5. Step 3: p1=8, p2=6. Step 4: p1=null → reset to headA=5, p2=null → reset to headB=7. Step 5: p1=6, p2=8. Step 6: p1=null, p2=null → both null.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Equalizing Path Lengths via List Switching
ladders.desc: The insight that switching lists makes pointers travel equal total distances to find intersection.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is the mathematical insight that by having each pointer traverse both lists (one starting from list A then B, the other from B then A), they cover the same combined distance (length A + length B). If there's an intersection, the pointers will meet at that node because the distance from each start to the intersection plus the distance from the intersection to the end of the other list becomes equal. Specifically, pointer1 travels: distance from headA to intersection + distance from headB to intersection. Pointer2 travels: distance from headB to intersection + distance from headA to intersection. These are identical, ensuring a meeting point. If no intersection exists, both pointers will traverse n+m nodes and become null simultaneously. The intuition relies on the property that the intersection node is common to both lists, so by traversing the full cycle, pointers synchronize. Time and space analysis is conceptual here, but it leads to O(n+m) time and O(1) space in implementation. This connects to L1 by providing the reasoning behind the pointer reset behavior. Common mistakes include not understanding why this works mathematically or misapplying it to non-intersecting cases. Real code pattern is derived from this logic but not implemented here.

examples.input: List A length=3 (nodes 1,2,3), List B length=2 (nodes 4,3), intersection at node 3
examples.trace: Pointer A path: 1→2→3 (A part) then 4→3 (B part) = 5 nodes. Pointer B path: 4→3 (B part) then 1→2→3 (A part) = 5 nodes. Both reach node 3 simultaneously.

examples.input: List A length=2 (nodes 5,6), List B length=2 (nodes 7,8), no intersection
examples.trace: Pointer A path: 5→6 (A) then 7→8 (B) = 4 nodes to null. Pointer B path: 7→8 (B) then 5→6 (A) = 4 nodes to null. Both become null together.

testCases.input: A length=4, B length=3, intersect at node 7
A length=5, B length=5, intersect at node 10
A length=1, B length=1, same node
A length=3, B length=0, no intersection
A length=2, B length=4, intersect at node 5
A length=6, B length=6, no intersection
A length=0, B length=2, no intersection
A length=7, B length=3, intersect at node 9
A length=1, B length=3, intersect at node 2
A length=8, B length=8, intersect at node 12

ladders.title: Building Block — Traversing Two Linked Lists with Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Simultaneously iterate through two linked lists using separate pointers.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves using two pointers to traverse two linked lists at the same time. The intuition is to compare nodes or perform operations across both lists in sync. The algorithm initializes a pointer for each list at their heads. In a loop, both pointers advance one step per iteration until one or both reach the end. This is fundamental for many linked list problems where you need to process two lists together. Time complexity is O(min(n,m)) if stopping at the shorter list's end, or O(max(n,m)) if continuing. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 by being the basic traversal mechanism before adding the reset logic. Common mistakes include not checking for null pointers before advancing or mishandling lists of different lengths. Real code pattern: p1, p2 = headA, headB; while p1 and p2: # process p1 and p2; p1 = p1.next; p2 = p2.next.

examples.input: List A: 1→2→3, List B: 4→5→6
examples.trace: Start: p1=1, p2=4. Step 1: p1=2, p2=5. Step 2: p1=3, p2=6. Step 3: p1=null, p2=null.
Start: p1=7, p2=11. Step 1: p1=8, p2=12. Step 2: p1=9, p2=null.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Advancing a Pointer in a Linked List
ladders.desc: Move a pointer from one node to the next in a singly linked list.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves traversing a linked list by moving a pointer from the current node to its next node. In a singly linked list, each node contains a value and a reference (or pointer) to the next node. To advance, you update the pointer to point to the next node using the 'next' reference. The intuition is that linked lists are sequential data structures, and advancing is how you iterate through them. The algorithm is simple: if the current pointer is not null, set it to current.next. If it's null, you've reached the end of the list. Time complexity is O(1) per advancement, and space is O(1). This connects to L3 as the fundamental step in traversing multiple lists. Common mistakes include trying to access 'next' on a null pointer (causing errors) or not updating the pointer correctly. Real code pattern: if pointer: pointer = pointer.next else: # handle end of list.

examples.input: Pointer at node with value 5 in list: 5→10→15
examples.trace: Start: pointer points to node(5, next=node10). After advancement: pointer = pointer.next, so now points to node(10, next=node15).

examples.input: Pointer at node with value 20 in list: 20→null (end of list)
examples.trace: Start: pointer points to node(20, next=null). After advancement: pointer = pointer.next = null.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Linked List Node Structure and Null Termination
ladders.desc: Understand that a linked list is made of nodes with value and next pointer, ending with null.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces the basic structure of a singly linked list. A linked list is a linear data structure where each element, called a node, contains two parts: a value (or data) and a reference (or pointer) to the next node in the sequence. The list starts with a head pointer that points to the first node. The last node has its next pointer set to null, indicating the end of the list. The intuition is that linked lists allow dynamic memory allocation and efficient insertions/deletions compared to arrays, but have sequential access. Nodes are connected via pointers, so traversing requires following these pointers from one node to the next. Time complexity for access is O(n), but operations at the head are O(1). Space complexity is O(n) for n nodes. This connects to L4 as the structure on which pointer advancement operates. Common mistakes include confusing null with a node or not initializing pointers properly. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.next = None.

examples.input: Create a linked list with nodes: 3→7→11
examples.trace: Start with head pointing to node1. Node1 has val=3 and next points to node2. Node2 has val=7 and next points to node3. Node3 has val=11 and next is null, marking the end.

examples.input: Create a linked list with a single node: 5
examples.trace: Head points to node1. Node1 has val=5 and next is null, so it's both the first and last node.

testCases.expected: Four nodes, last next is null
Three nodes, all val=1, last next null
